title: Paediatric Sepsis
oneLiner: Life-threatening organ dysfunction from infection; rapid recognition and escalation save lives in children.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Meningococcal sepsis (with Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome):**  
  *Single finding – a non-blanching purpuric rash (especially if >2 lesions or rapid spread), plus haemodynamic instability.*  
  ⇒ Immediate IV antibiotics (cefotaxime/ceftriaxone) and massive fluid resuscitation; consider hydrocortisone for adrenal crisis.

- **Septic shock with unrecognised hypovolaemia:**  
  *Single finding – a child with tachycardia, prolonged capillary refill (>2 s) and hypotension (late sign in children) who does not improve after 40 mL/kg fluids.*  
  ⇒ This is not a stable patient – they need inotropes (e.g. adrenaline infusion) and ICU transfer; do not keep repeating fluid boluses >60 mL/kg without senior review.

- **Obstructed airway from severe croup or epiglottitis (mimicking respiratory sepsis):**  
  *Single finding – stridor at rest, drooling, or toxic appearance with an inability to lie flat; do NOT examine the throat.*  
  ⇒ Secure airway immediately – call anaesthetics/ENT; antibiotics later.

- **Cardiogenic shock (mimicking distributive shock):**  
  *Single finding – gallop rhythm, hepatomegaly, and/or raised JVP in a child with respiratory distress; *cold* shock with a narrow pulse pressure.*  
  ⇒ Do NOT give large fluid boluses – seek echo and cardiology review.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Fever or hypothermia, lethargy/irritability, poor feeding, tachycardia, tachypnoea  
  - In younger infants: grunting, apnoea, mottling, poor tone  
  - Older children: altered mental state, headache, stiff neck (meningitis), reduced urine output

- **Key exam findings:**  
  - General: toxic or unwell appearance, drowsy or floppy  
  - Cardiovascular: tachycardia, prolonged capillary refill (>2 s), weak or bounding pulses, hypotension (late), mottled skin  
  - Respiratory: tachypnoea, grunting, increased work of breathing  
  - Neurological: GCS <15, neck stiffness, bulging fontanelle (infants)  
  - Skin: petechiae/purpura, non-blanching rash, cellulitis, surgical site redness
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Sepsis (bacterial/viral/fungal)** – systemic inflammatory response + suspected infection  
- **Cardiogenic shock** – looks shocked but has signs of heart failure (gallop, hepatomegaly, narrow pulse pressure); **splitter:** normal lactate but raised BNP  
- **Anaphylaxis** – urticaria, stridor/wheeze, hypotension with a clear trigger; **splitter:** absence of fever  
- **Toxic shock syndrome (staphylococcal/streptococcal)** – fever, hypotension, diffuse rash, desquamation; **splitter:** full-thickness red rash resembling sunburn  
- **Inborn errors of metabolism (neonates/infants)** – metabolic acidosis, hypoglycaemia, hyperammonaemia; **splitter:** severe acidosis with normal perfusion  
- **Adrenal insufficiency** – chronic lethargy, hyperpigmentation, hyponatraemia; **splitter:** refractory hypotension despite fluids  
- **Surgical abdomen (e.g. volvulus)** – bilious vomiting, distension, pain; **splitter:** abdominal findings dominate
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**  
  - Capillary refill, heart rate, BP, respiratory rate, oxygen saturations, temperature  
  - Blood glucose (immediate; any value <3 mmol/L treat)  
  - Blood gas (for lactate, pH, base excess)  
  - Monitor: urine output (catheter if shocked), continuous SpO₂

- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, CRP, procalcitonin (if available), U&E, creatinine, LFTs, blood glucose  
  - Coagulation screen (DIC can develop rapidly)  
  - Lactate (venous or arterial)  
  - Blood cultures (×2 from separate sites before first dose antibiotics)  
  - Consider: meningococcal/enterovirus PCR, HSV PCR if encephalitis, beta-HCG (adolescent girls)

- **Imaging:**  
  - CXR (for pneumonia, ARDS, cardiomegaly)  
  - Urgent echo (if cardiogenic shock suspected, or in persistent shock after 40 mL/kg)

- **Special tests:**  
  - LP if meningitis suspected and no contraindications (raised ICP, coagulopathy, shock); send for culture, PCR, glucose, protein  
  - Urine dip + culture (consider all ages)

- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**  
  - **NICE Sepsis in Children (traffic light system)** – Red flags: looks seriously unwell, non-blanching rash, GCS <15/7, not responding to social stimuli, grunting, apnoea, oxygen saturations ≤90%.  
  - **qSOFA is NOT validated in children** – use PEWS (Paediatric Early Warning Score) or local track-and-trigger tool.

- **Never-miss test:**  
  - **Blood cultures before first antibiotic dose** (ideally from two peripheral sites). Missing this risks losing aetiological diagnosis and tailoring therapy.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - **ABC:** high-flow oxygen, secure IV/IO access (two large-bore), continuous monitoring  
  - **Fluids:** 10-20 mL/kg crystalloid (0.9% sodium chloride) bolus over 10-15 min, reassess. Repeat up to 40-60 mL/kg total if shock persists. **After 40 mL/kg, discuss with senior before continuing.**  
  - **Antibiotics:** Give **within 1 hour of recognition** – IV cefotaxime (50 mg/kg) or ceftriaxone (80 mg/kg). Add acyclovir (10 mg/kg) if HSV encephalitis suspected. Add antistaphylococcal cover (e.g. flucloxacillin) if line infection or skin source.  
  - **Source control:** Remove any central line if thought infected; drain abscess if accessible.

- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Continue IV antibiotics based on microbiology results  
  - Daily review: CRP trend, lactate clearance, clinical improvement  
  - Switch to oral antibiotics when afebrile >48 h and clinically improving (guided by local policy)  
  - Monitor end-organ function: renal, hepatic, respiratory  
  - Consider echocardiogram if prolonged shock or murmur

- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Hearing assessment (especially if meningitis)  
  - Follow-up with paediatrician for neurodevelopmental review  
  - Vaccination catch-up (e.g. meningococcal B/C if not immunised)

- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - **Fluids:** maintenance IV fluids (0.9% sodium chloride + 5% glucose, based on age/weight) if not taking orally  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** not routine in children unless risk factors (CVC, malignancy); consider low molecular weight heparin with senior input  
  - **Diet/NBM:** NBM if for LP or theatre; otherwise encourage oral intake as able  
  - **Observation frequency:** PEWS/NEWS2 – hourly initially, then 2-hourly once stable; continuous SpO₂
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line antibiotics:**  
  - **Cefotaxime:** 50 mg/kg IV every 6-8 hours (max 12 g/day)  
  - **Ceftriaxone:** 80 mg/kg IV once daily (max 4 g/day) – *caution in neonates and hyperbilirubinaemia*  
- **Alternatives:**  
  - Penicillin-allergic: consider ciprofloxacin (10 mg/kg IV 12 hourly) + clindamycin (10 mg/kg IV 8 hourly), but seek microbiology advice.  
  - *If uncertain dose: [check local policy]*  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - Ceftriaxone – do not give with calcium-containing IV fluids (risk of precipitation)  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - No major adjustment needed for cefotaxime/ceftriaxone in mild-moderate renal impairment; if severe (GFR <30), reduce dose of cefotaxime  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Giving a cephalosporin without checking for severe penicillin allergy** (anaphylaxis risk) – use alternative class if known anaphylaxis.  
  - **Fluid bolus in unrecognised cardiogenic shock** – can cause pulmonary oedema.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Septic shock, DIC, ARDS, multi-organ failure, acute kidney injury  
  - Meningitis/encephalitis, cerebral oedema, seizures  
  - Compartment syndrome (e.g. meningococcal purpura fulminans)  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Limb ischaemia/amputation, hearing loss, neurological deficit, post-streptococcal nephritis
sections.Prognosis:
- Mortality in paediatric sepsis ≈ 5-10%; higher (up to 30%) in septic shock with multi-organ failure.  
- Full recovery expected in most survivors, but neurodevelopmental sequelae may follow meningitis.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **DO** give first dose of antibiotics within 1 hour of recognition – time to antibiotics is the strongest predictor of mortality.  
- **DO** use the NICE traffic light system to risk-stratify children with fever – it detects 96% of serious bacterial infections.  
- **DO** involve paediatric ICU early – if shock persists after 40 mL/kg, call the retrieval team.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **DON’T** delay antibiotics to get cultures – take cultures immediately after starting antibiotics if needed, but never wait >1 hour.  
- **DON’T** give more than 60 mL/kg of fluid without senior review – risk of fluid overload and ARDS.  
- **DON’T** forget to check blood glucose – hypoglycaemia worsens neurological outcome.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - New or worsening non-blanching rash  
  - Drowsiness, confusion, or stiff neck  
  - Not drinking or passing urine for 12 hours  
  - Fever >38°C persisting after 48 hours of antibiotics  
- **Follow-up:** GP in 24-48 hours; paediatric outpatient review within 1-2 weeks if ongoing concerns
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior immediately if:**  
  - Shock persists after 40 mL/kg of fluids (lactate rising, hypotension)  
  - GCS <12 or falling  
  - Need for inotropic support (e.g. adrenaline infusion)  
  - Any new purpuric rash  
  - PEWS/NEWS2 ≥5 or upward trend  
- **Call ICU for:**  
  - Septic shock unresponsive to first-line management  
  - Requiring >60 mL/kg fluid or inotropes  
  - Respiratory failure (need for NIV or ventilation)  
  - Any child needing urgent transfer from district general hospital to tertiary PICU

rawMarkdown:
## Paediatric Sepsis
**One-liner:** Life-threatening organ dysfunction from infection; rapid recognition and escalation save lives in children.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Meningococcal sepsis (with Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome):**  
  *Single finding – a non-blanching purpuric rash (especially if >2 lesions or rapid spread), plus haemodynamic instability.*  
  ⇒ Immediate IV antibiotics (cefotaxime/ceftriaxone) and massive fluid resuscitation; consider hydrocortisone for adrenal crisis.

- **Septic shock with unrecognised hypovolaemia:**  
  *Single finding – a child with tachycardia, prolonged capillary refill (>2 s) and hypotension (late sign in children) who does not improve after 40 mL/kg fluids.*  
  ⇒ This is not a stable patient – they need inotropes (e.g. adrenaline infusion) and ICU transfer; do not keep repeating fluid boluses >60 mL/kg without senior review.

- **Obstructed airway from severe croup or epiglottitis (mimicking respiratory sepsis):**  
  *Single finding – stridor at rest, drooling, or toxic appearance with an inability to lie flat; do NOT examine the throat.*  
  ⇒ Secure airway immediately – call anaesthetics/ENT; antibiotics later.

- **Cardiogenic shock (mimicking distributive shock):**  
  *Single finding – gallop rhythm, hepatomegaly, and/or raised JVP in a child with respiratory distress; *cold* shock with a narrow pulse pressure.*  
  ⇒ Do NOT give large fluid boluses – seek echo and cardiology review.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Fever or hypothermia, lethargy/irritability, poor feeding, tachycardia, tachypnoea  
  - In younger infants: grunting, apnoea, mottling, poor tone  
  - Older children: altered mental state, headache, stiff neck (meningitis), reduced urine output

- **Key exam findings:**  
  - General: toxic or unwell appearance, drowsy or floppy  
  - Cardiovascular: tachycardia, prolonged capillary refill (>2 s), weak or bounding pulses, hypotension (late), mottled skin  
  - Respiratory: tachypnoea, grunting, increased work of breathing  
  - Neurological: GCS <15, neck stiffness, bulging fontanelle (infants)  
  - Skin: petechiae/purpura, non-blanching rash, cellulitis, surgical site redness

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Sepsis (bacterial/viral/fungal)** – systemic inflammatory response + suspected infection  
- **Cardiogenic shock** – looks shocked but has signs of heart failure (gallop, hepatomegaly, narrow pulse pressure); **splitter:** normal lactate but raised BNP  
- **Anaphylaxis** – urticaria, stridor/wheeze, hypotension with a clear trigger; **splitter:** absence of fever  
- **Toxic shock syndrome (staphylococcal/streptococcal)** – fever, hypotension, diffuse rash, desquamation; **splitter:** full-thickness red rash resembling sunburn  
- **Inborn errors of metabolism (neonates/infants)** – metabolic acidosis, hypoglycaemia, hyperammonaemia; **splitter:** severe acidosis with normal perfusion  
- **Adrenal insufficiency** – chronic lethargy, hyperpigmentation, hyponatraemia; **splitter:** refractory hypotension despite fluids  
- **Surgical abdomen (e.g. volvulus)** – bilious vomiting, distension, pain; **splitter:** abdominal findings dominate

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**  
  - Capillary refill, heart rate, BP, respiratory rate, oxygen saturations, temperature  
  - Blood glucose (immediate; any value <3 mmol/L treat)  
  - Blood gas (for lactate, pH, base excess)  
  - Monitor: urine output (catheter if shocked), continuous SpO₂

- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, CRP, procalcitonin (if available), U&E, creatinine, LFTs, blood glucose  
  - Coagulation screen (DIC can develop rapidly)  
  - Lactate (venous or arterial)  
  - Blood cultures (×2 from separate sites before first dose antibiotics)  
  - Consider: meningococcal/enterovirus PCR, HSV PCR if encephalitis, beta-HCG (adolescent girls)

- **Imaging:**  
  - CXR (for pneumonia, ARDS, cardiomegaly)  
  - Urgent echo (if cardiogenic shock suspected, or in persistent shock after 40 mL/kg)

- **Special tests:**  
  - LP if meningitis suspected and no contraindications (raised ICP, coagulopathy, shock); send for culture, PCR, glucose, protein  
  - Urine dip + culture (consider all ages)

- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**  
  - **NICE Sepsis in Children (traffic light system)** – Red flags: looks seriously unwell, non-blanching rash, GCS <15/7, not responding to social stimuli, grunting, apnoea, oxygen saturations ≤90%.  
  - **qSOFA is NOT validated in children** – use PEWS (Paediatric Early Warning Score) or local track-and-trigger tool.

- **Never-miss test:**  
  - **Blood cultures before first antibiotic dose** (ideally from two peripheral sites). Missing this risks losing aetiological diagnosis and tailoring therapy.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - **ABC:** high-flow oxygen, secure IV/IO access (two large-bore), continuous monitoring  
  - **Fluids:** 10-20 mL/kg crystalloid (0.9% sodium chloride) bolus over 10-15 min, reassess. Repeat up to 40-60 mL/kg total if shock persists. **After 40 mL/kg, discuss with senior before continuing.**  
  - **Antibiotics:** Give **within 1 hour of recognition** – IV cefotaxime (50 mg/kg) or ceftriaxone (80 mg/kg). Add acyclovir (10 mg/kg) if HSV encephalitis suspected. Add antistaphylococcal cover (e.g. flucloxacillin) if line infection or skin source.  
  - **Source control:** Remove any central line if thought infected; drain abscess if accessible.

- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Continue IV antibiotics based on microbiology results  
  - Daily review: CRP trend, lactate clearance, clinical improvement  
  - Switch to oral antibiotics when afebrile >48 h and clinically improving (guided by local policy)  
  - Monitor end-organ function: renal, hepatic, respiratory  
  - Consider echocardiogram if prolonged shock or murmur

- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Hearing assessment (especially if meningitis)  
  - Follow-up with paediatrician for neurodevelopmental review  
  - Vaccination catch-up (e.g. meningococcal B/C if not immunised)

- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - **Fluids:** maintenance IV fluids (0.9% sodium chloride + 5% glucose, based on age/weight) if not taking orally  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** not routine in children unless risk factors (CVC, malignancy); consider low molecular weight heparin with senior input  
  - **Diet/NBM:** NBM if for LP or theatre; otherwise encourage oral intake as able  
  - **Observation frequency:** PEWS/NEWS2 – hourly initially, then 2-hourly once stable; continuous SpO₂

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line antibiotics:**  
  - **Cefotaxime:** 50 mg/kg IV every 6-8 hours (max 12 g/day)  
  - **Ceftriaxone:** 80 mg/kg IV once daily (max 4 g/day) – *caution in neonates and hyperbilirubinaemia*  
- **Alternatives:**  
  - Penicillin-allergic: consider ciprofloxacin (10 mg/kg IV 12 hourly) + clindamycin (10 mg/kg IV 8 hourly), but seek microbiology advice.  
  - *If uncertain dose: [check local policy]*  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - Ceftriaxone – do not give with calcium-containing IV fluids (risk of precipitation)  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - No major adjustment needed for cefotaxime/ceftriaxone in mild-moderate renal impairment; if severe (GFR <30), reduce dose of cefotaxime  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Giving a cephalosporin without checking for severe penicillin allergy** (anaphylaxis risk) – use alternative class if known anaphylaxis.  
  - **Fluid bolus in unrecognised cardiogenic shock** – can cause pulmonary oedema.

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Septic shock, DIC, ARDS, multi-organ failure, acute kidney injury  
  - Meningitis/encephalitis, cerebral oedema, seizures  
  - Compartment syndrome (e.g. meningococcal purpura fulminans)  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Limb ischaemia/amputation, hearing loss, neurological deficit, post-streptococcal nephritis

### Prognosis
- Mortality in paediatric sepsis ≈ 5-10%; higher (up to 30%) in septic shock with multi-organ failure.  
- Full recovery expected in most survivors, but neurodevelopmental sequelae may follow meningitis.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **DO** give first dose of antibiotics within 1 hour of recognition – time to antibiotics is the strongest predictor of mortality.  
- **DO** use the NICE traffic light system to risk-stratify children with fever – it detects 96% of serious bacterial infections.  
- **DO** involve paediatric ICU early – if shock persists after 40 mL/kg, call the retrieval team.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **DON’T** delay antibiotics to get cultures – take cultures immediately after starting antibiotics if needed, but never wait >1 hour.  
- **DON’T** give more than 60 mL/kg of fluid without senior review – risk of fluid overload and ARDS.  
- **DON’T** forget to check blood glucose – hypoglycaemia worsens neurological outcome.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - New or worsening non-blanching rash  
  - Drowsiness, confusion, or stiff neck  
  - Not drinking or passing urine for 12 hours  
  - Fever >38°C persisting after 48 hours of antibiotics  
- **Follow-up:** GP in 24-48 hours; paediatric outpatient review within 1-2 weeks if ongoing concerns

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior immediately if:**  
  - Shock persists after 40 mL/kg of fluids (lactate rising, hypotension)  
  - GCS <12 or falling  
  - Need for inotropic support (e.g. adrenaline infusion)  
  - Any new purpuric rash  
  - PEWS/NEWS2 ≥5 or upward trend  
- **Call ICU for:**  
  - Septic shock unresponsive to first-line management  
  - Requiring >60 mL/kg fluid or inotropes  
  - Respiratory failure (need for NIV or ventilation)  
  - Any child needing urgent transfer from district general hospital to tertiary PICU
